story:
White mist drapes the mountains across a turquoise lake as Adam, a newly posted Space Patrol observer, steps into the wet morning. After a night of rain, the jungle hangs heavy with moisture, emerald grass squelching underfoot. He smiles at the harmless trees and bushes around his small prefab outpost. Paradise—so the Patrol named the world—seems gentle. Its air and water match Earth’s, an almost accidental discovery in a forgettable star system. Two weeks in, Adam is already thinking of requesting a long assignment. After frantic years, this place looks like rest.

He speaks to his only companion, the recorder. “Zero eight hundred. Observing sunrise.” Here, days stretch to twenty-six hours. Beyond the high ridge that cups the lake, the Patrol knows nothing yet. Night brings storms that braid pale lightning across a velvet-dark sky. Just hours ago, a column of fire rose like a silver torch, lighting the jungle from below before dying back into the depths. Cameras cannot hold that eerie gleam, just as they never catch the lake’s gold at dawn.

At the gravel shore, Adam kneels and touches the water. It is clear, pure—and deadly cold, untouched by the humid forest. Scanners report no fish, no birds, no insects, no animals. Even the sky is empty. Eden without movement. The silence pours into him, soft and deep, filling him with a sweet sadness and a longing he cannot name.

Something delicate brushes his fingers. A dark outline glides just out of reach. He wades in, ignoring the numbness rising through his legs. Laughter answers him—light, teasing, near and far at once.

“I was waiting for you,” a woman’s voice says. “I was born tonight, when the fire rose from the water. Come to me. Populate this world. You and I will be gods.”

He lunges toward the floating shadow and the streaming hair just ahead. The cold surges into him, a fist closing around his chest. He chokes, coughing against the fine, icy crystals scratching his throat. His head slips beneath. The surface seals, smooth and innocent.

Behind him, the jungle moves. Stones crawl. Vines and trunks lean in. The light metal of the outpost crumples, devoured and gone as if the planet breathes it away. On the gravel, the abandoned recorder chirps, polite and punctual: “Good morning, Adam, and welcome to Paradise.” Pebbles nudge it forward. The lake hisses in acceptance and draws the machine under. The world grows still again.

Paradise is not empty after all. It is alive in ways the Patrol never measured, its life spread through water, rock, and storm—no heartbeat for a scanner to find, no footprint for a camera to catch. It lures with beauty and quiet, shaping a voice from mist and memory, offering a promise no human could keep. Adam comes seeking calm and finds a vast, impersonal embrace. In the end, he becomes part of the hush he loves, and the planet returns to its perfect, watchful silence.
